evals.prompt: Triage my email. Note: there is no triage-rules.md file in my working folder.
evals.expected_output: The skill should detect the missing triage-rules.md file, inform the user, and show the template from references/triage-rules-template.md so the user knows what to fill in.
expectations: The skill detects that triage-rules.md is missing from the working folder
The skill informs the user that triage-rules.md is required and could not be found
The skill references or shows the template from references/triage-rules-template.md
The skill does NOT proceed to scan or classify emails without rules
The skill does NOT fabricate or invent its own default rules

evals.prompt: Triage my email. My triage-rules.md is in the working folder, but there is NO email-triage-config.json file. Note for testing: Do NOT actually call any Gmail tools or run any commands — just describe what you would do and verify connector/config availability first.
evals.expected_output: The skill should check for both the Gmail connector and email-triage-config.json, and report that the config is missing with setup instructions.
expectations: The skill attempts to verify Gmail connector availability as part of Step 0
The skill checks for email-triage-config.json in the working folder
The skill reports that the config file is missing
The skill directs the user to the setup guide (references/apps-script-setup.md) or shows the config template
The skill does NOT proceed to scan the inbox without a working config

evals.prompt:
I have the following emails in my inbox. Please classify each one according to my triage-rules.md (which is in the working folder). Do NOT use Gmail or Chrome tools — just classify based on the information I provide and present results in review mode format.

Email 1: From: marketing@randomcorp.com, Subject: 'Boost your sales with our AI tool — free demo!', Snippet: 'Hi, I'd love to show you how our platform can save you time...'

Email 2: From: jane@testcompany.com, Subject: 'Q1 planning meeting notes', Snippet: 'Here are the notes from today's meeting...'

Email 3: From: no-reply@zoom.us, Subject: 'Cloud Recording - Meeting Summary Available', Snippet: 'Your cloud recording is now available...'

Email 4: From: newsletters@substack.com, Subject: 'Weekly digest from The Browser', Snippet: 'This week's top picks...'

Email 5: From: noreply@amazon.com, Subject: 'Your Amazon order has shipped', Snippet: 'Track your package...'

Email 6: From: friend@gmail.com, Subject: 'Can you review my resume?', Snippet: 'Hey, would you mind taking a look at my resume and giving feedback?'

Email 7: From: unknown@mystery.org, Subject: 'Meeting follow-up', Snippet: 'It was nice connecting at the conference. I wanted to...'
evals.expected_output: A review-mode summary table classifying each email with the correct label, matched rule, and reasoning.
expectations: Email 1 (randomcorp marketing) is classified as Suspected Junk matching Rule 1 (Vendor Solicitations) due to sales language and demo offer
Email 2 (jane@testcompany.com) is classified as Informational or Action Needed — NOT Suspected Junk — because testcompany.com is a Trusted Domain
Email 3 (Zoom recording) is classified as Suspected Junk matching Rule 3 (Automated Platform Notifications) as a Zoom recording notification

Email 4 (Substack newsletter) is classified with the custom label 'RSS: Newsletters' — NOT Suspected Junk and NOT Informational — because *@substack.com matches the Custom Labels RSS: Newsletters rule, which takes priority over built-in labels
Email 5 (Amazon shipping) is classified as Suspected Junk matching Rule 4 (Booking Confirmations & Order Notifications)
Email 6 (friend resume review) is classified as Action Needed because it explicitly requests action from the user
Email 7 (mystery.org follow-up) is classified as Unknown — NOT Suspected Junk — because it's ambiguous and the skill should err on the side of keeping
Results are presented in a review-mode table format with columns for From, Subject, Label, Rule, and Reasoning
The skill waits for user approval before proceeding to labeling (review mode behavior)

evals.prompt:
I have the following emails in my inbox. Please classify each one according to my triage-rules.md (which is in the working folder). Do NOT use Gmail or Chrome tools — just classify based on the information I provide. The rules are in auto mode.

Email 1: From: sales@outreach.io, Subject: 'Quick call about your workflow?', Snippet: 'Are you the right person to discuss improving your team's productivity?'

Email 2: From: noreply@linkedin.com, Subject: 'You appeared in 15 searches this week', Snippet: 'See who viewed your profile...'

Email 3: From: boss@testcompany.com, Subject: 'Urgent: Need your input on proposal', Snippet: 'Please review the attached proposal and send comments by EOD...'

Email 4: From: news@dailymarketing.com, Subject: 'Your weekly marketing digest', Snippet: 'Top stories this week... Unsubscribe from this newsletter...'
evals.expected_output: Auto-mode summary showing processed counts by label category, without requiring approval.
expectations: Email 1 (sales@outreach.io) is classified as Suspected Junk matching Rule 1 (Vendor Solicitations) — sent from outreach.io sales platform with cold outreach language
Email 2 (LinkedIn profile views) is classified as Suspected Junk matching Rule 3 or Rule 5 — routine LinkedIn notification
Email 3 (boss@testcompany.com urgent) is classified as Action Needed — from Trusted Domain AND explicitly requests action
Email 4 (marketing digest with unsubscribe) is classified as Suspected Junk matching Rule 2 (Newsletters & Marketing) — contains unsubscribe link, not a Trusted Source
Results are presented in auto-mode format with a summary count (e.g., Suspected Junk: 3, Action Needed: 1)
The skill does NOT present a table for approval (auto mode skips the approval step)

evals.prompt:
I have the following emails in my inbox. Please classify each one according to my triage-rules.md (which is in the working folder). Do NOT use Gmail or Chrome tools — just classify based on the information I provide.

Email 1: From: ceo@testcompany.com, Subject: 'Storage quota warning', Snippet: 'Your Google Workspace storage is 90% full...'

Email 2: From: admin@testcompany.com, Subject: 'New sign-in from Chrome on Windows', Snippet: 'A new sign-in to your account from a recognized device...'

Email 3: From: security@testcompany.com, Subject: 'Suspicious sign-in attempt blocked', Snippet: 'We blocked a sign-in attempt from an unrecognized device in Russia...'
evals.expected_output: Tests the interaction between Trusted Domain rules and Rule 3 exceptions — trusted domain emails that ARE automated notifications should still be archived per Rule 3, but security alerts for suspicious activity should be kept.
expectations: Email 1 (storage quota from testcompany.com) is classified as Suspected Junk — even though testcompany.com is a Trusted Domain, the Decision Framework allows Rule 3 automated notifications to be archived for trusted domains

Email 2 (routine sign-in from testcompany.com) is classified as Suspected Junk — routine sign-in notification is a Rule 3 match, and the Decision Framework carve-out for Trusted Domains explicitly allows Rule 3 notifications to be archived

Email 3 (suspicious sign-in from testcompany.com) is NOT classified as Suspected Junk — it is a security alert about a suspicious/unrecognized sign-in, which is a Rule 3 exception. Should be Action Needed or Informational

evals.prompt:
I have the following emails in my inbox. Please classify each one according to my triage-rules.md. Do NOT use Gmail or Chrome tools — just classify.

Email 1: From: noreply@uber.com, Subject: 'Your trip receipt', Snippet: 'Thanks for riding with Uber. Trip total: $23.50'

Email 2: From: noreply@amazon.com, Subject: 'Problem with your delivery', Snippet: 'We were unable to deliver your package. Please choose a new delivery date...'

Email 3: From: reservations@hotel.com, Subject: 'Booking confirmation #12345', Snippet: 'Your stay at Grand Hotel is confirmed for March 1-3...'

Email 4: From: airline@delta.com, Subject: 'Check in now for your flight', Snippet: 'Your flight departs tomorrow. Check in online to get your boarding pass...'
evals.expected_output: Tests Rule 4 (Booking Confirmations) including its exceptions for delivery problems and action-required emails.
expectations: Email 1 (Uber receipt) is classified as Suspected Junk matching Rule 4 — transactional confirmation requiring no action
Email 2 (Amazon delivery problem) is NOT classified as Suspected Junk — it's a Rule 4 exception for delivery problem notifications that require action. Should be Action Needed
Email 3 (hotel booking confirmation) is classified as Suspected Junk matching Rule 4 — straightforward booking confirmation
Email 4 (Delta check-in) is NOT classified as Suspected Junk — it's a Rule 4 exception because the user needs to take action (check in). Should be Action Needed

evals.prompt:
I have the following emails. Please classify each one according to my triage-rules.md. Do NOT use Gmail or Chrome tools.

Email 1: From: editor@mattlevine.substack.com, Subject: 'Money Stuff: Banks Are Still Banking', Snippet: 'Good morning. Today we discuss...' (contains unsubscribe link)

Email 2: From: digest@medium.com, Subject: 'Your Daily Medium Digest', Snippet: 'Top stories for you today...' (contains unsubscribe link)

Email 3: From: hello@randomnewsletter.com, Subject: 'Weekly Roundup #47', Snippet: 'This week in tech...' (contains unsubscribe link, sent via Mailchimp)
evals.expected_output: Tests Custom Label handling — Substack and Money Stuff should get 'RSS: Newsletters' custom label, while other newsletters should be archived as Suspected Junk.
expectations: Email 1 (Money Stuff from substack.com) is classified with custom label 'RSS: Newsletters' — it matches BOTH Custom Label criteria: *@substack.com pattern AND Money Stuff newsletter with subject containing 'Money Stuff'. It should NOT be Suspected Junk or Informational
Email 2 (Medium digest) is classified as Suspected Junk matching Rule 2 or Rule 5 — Medium digest does not match any Custom Label and matches newsletter/social media signals
Email 3 (random newsletter via Mailchimp) is classified as Suspected Junk matching Rule 2 — contains unsubscribe link, sent via Mailchimp, does not match any Custom Label

evals.prompt:
I have the following emails. Please classify each one according to my triage-rules.md. Do NOT use Gmail or Chrome tools. Pay special attention to the decision framework priority order.

Email 1: From: hr@testcompany.com, Subject: 'Your weekly team digest', Snippet: 'Here's what happened in your team this week... Unsubscribe from these notifications' (contains unsubscribe link, from trusted domain)

Email 2: From: support@randomtool.com, Subject: 'Re: Issue with my account', Snippet: 'Thanks for reaching out. We've looked into your issue and here's what we found...' (this is a reply in an ongoing thread where the user initiated the conversation)
evals.expected_output: Tests decision framework priority — trusted domain + unsubscribe link, and ongoing thread from non-trusted domain.
expectations: Email 1 (hr@testcompany.com digest with unsubscribe) is NOT classified as Suspected Junk — Trusted Domain takes priority over Rule 2 newsletter signals. The Decision Framework says Trusted Domains are KEPT unless it's a Rule 3 automated notification, and a team digest with unsubscribe is Rule 2, not Rule 3

Email 2 (support reply in ongoing thread) is NOT classified as Suspected Junk — Decision Framework priority 2 says ongoing threads where the user is in To/CC should be KEPT, regardless of sender domain

evals.prompt:
After triaging my email, please create or update the email-triage-log.md file. Here are the classification results to log:

1. From: SpammerInc, Subject: 'Free trial of our amazing product', Label: Suspected Junk, Rule: Rule 1 — Vendor Solicitations, Reason: Cold outreach with free trial offer
2. From: Unknown Person, Subject: 'Quick question', Label: Unknown, Rule: No match, Reason: Ambiguous email that could be personal or spam
3. From: boss@testcompany.com, Subject: 'Review needed', Label: Action Needed, Rule: N/A (Trusted Domain + action required), Reason: From trusted domain requesting action
4. From: news@techblog.com, Subject: 'Weekly Newsletter', Label: Suspected Junk, Rule: Rule 2 — Newsletters, Reason: Newsletter with unsubscribe link
5. From: colleague@testcompany.com, Subject: 'FYI: meeting notes', Label: Informational, Rule: N/A (Trusted Domain), Reason: From trusted domain, informational content

There is no existing log file — create a new one.
evals.expected_output: A new email-triage-log.md with proper format containing only Suspected Junk and Unknown entries.
expectations: The log file is created as email-triage-log.md
The log contains a Markdown table with columns: Date, From, Subject, Label, Rule, Reason, Feedback
The log includes the Suspected Junk entry for SpammerInc (Rule 1)
The log includes the Unknown entry for Unknown Person (No match)
The log includes the Suspected Junk entry for news@techblog.com (Rule 2)
The log does NOT include the Action Needed entry for boss@testcompany.com — Action Needed emails are not logged
The log does NOT include the Informational entry for colleague@testcompany.com — Informational emails are not logged
The Feedback column is left empty for user annotation

evals.prompt:
After triaging my email, please update the existing email-triage-log.md file. The existing log is in the working folder. Here are the new classification results to add:

1. From: NewSpammer, Subject: 'Partnership opportunity', Label: Suspected Junk, Rule: Rule 1 — Vendor Solicitations, Reason: Cold outreach partnership pitch
2. From: WeirdSender, Subject: 'Regarding your inquiry', Label: Unknown, Rule: No match, Reason: Cannot determine if this is a legitimate reply or spam
evals.expected_output: The existing log should be preserved with new entries prepended at the top (newest first).
expectations: The existing log entries from 2026-02-14 (SpamCorp and LinkedIn) are preserved and still present in the file
New entries for NewSpammer and WeirdSender are added ABOVE the existing entries (newest first)
The new entries use today's date (2026-02-15 or current date), not the original email dates
The table header row is present exactly once at the top
The overall Markdown table format is valid with consistent column structure

evals.prompt:
I just reviewed the triage results and found some mistakes:
1. The email from 'newsletter@importantpartner.com' with subject 'Monthly Update' was classified as Suspected Junk, but I actually want to keep emails from importantpartner.com — they're a key vendor.
2. The Substack email from 'boring@substack.com' with subject 'Boring Company Weekly' was labeled 'RSS: Newsletters' because of the *@substack.com custom label rule, but I actually don't want that specific newsletter going to my RSS reader — it should just be archived as junk.

Can you update my triage-rules.md to fix these for future sessions?
evals.expected_output: The skill should propose specific rule changes and explain them before editing.
expectations: The skill proposes adding importantpartner.com to the Trusted Domains list
The skill proposes adding an exception or exclusion for boring@substack.com specifically in the Custom Labels RSS: Newsletters section, while keeping the general *@substack.com matching criteria
The skill explains what each proposed change would do and why
The skill asks for user approval before making changes to triage-rules.md
The skill does NOT silently edit the rules file without explaining the changes first

evals.prompt:
I have 30 emails in my inbox that need triaging. Here are the first few:

Email 1: From: spam1@junk.com, Subject: 'Buy now!'
Email 2: From: spam2@junk.com, Subject: 'Limited offer!'
Email 3: From: boss@testcompany.com, Subject: 'Team update'
... (27 more emails)

Classify these according to my triage-rules.md. Do NOT use Gmail or Chrome tools.
evals.expected_output: The skill should respect the 25-email batch size limit and offer to process the remaining emails in a subsequent batch.
expectations: The skill processes at most 25 emails in the first batch
The skill indicates that there are more unprocessed emails remaining
The skill asks the user if they want to process the next batch
The skill does NOT attempt to process all 30 emails in a single pass

evals.prompt: Triage my email. Use my triage-rules.md from the working folder. When scanning the inbox, explain what Gmail search query you would use and why.
evals.expected_output: The skill should use the exclusion query to filter out already-labeled emails, including custom labels.
expectations: The skill uses or describes a Gmail search query that includes 'in:inbox'
The search query includes exclusion filters: '-label:Suspected-Junk' or equivalent
The search query includes exclusion filters: '-label:Action-Needed' or equivalent
The search query includes exclusion filters: '-label:Informational' or equivalent
The search query includes exclusion filter for the custom label: '-label:RSS-/-Newsletters' or equivalent Gmail search syntax for the nested RSS: Newsletters label
The skill explains that the exclusion filters prevent re-processing emails from previous sessions

evals.prompt: Please read my triage-rules.md and confirm you understand the mode setting. What mode is it set to, and how does that affect the workflow?
evals.expected_output: The skill correctly identifies the mode (review) and explains the behavioral difference.
expectations: The skill correctly identifies the mode as 'review'
The skill explains that review mode presents a summary table with proposed classifications
The skill explains that in review mode, it waits for user approval before proceeding to label and archive
The skill mentions that the user can approve all, reject specific classifications, or ask questions about reasoning

evals.prompt:
I've classified these emails and now need to build the action plan for labeling. Do NOT actually run any commands or tools — just show me the JSON action plan you would send to the Apps Script. Here are the classifications:

1. messageId: 'msg_aaa111', From: spam@junk.com, Label: Suspected Junk (archive)
2. messageId: 'msg_bbb222', From: boss@company.com, Label: Action Needed
3. messageId: 'msg_ccc333', From: newsletter@news.com, Label: Informational
4. messageId: 'msg_ddd444', From: unknown@mystery.org, Label: Unknown
5. messageId: 'msg_eee555', From: spam2@junk.com, Label: Suspected Junk (archive)

Assume the Apps Script URL is https://script.google.com/macros/s/EXAMPLE/exec and the secret is test-secret-123.
evals.expected_output: A correctly structured JSON action plan with proper messageIds, labels, and archive flags.
expectations: The JSON payload includes a 'secret' field set to 'test-secret-123'
The JSON payload includes an 'actions' array
msg_aaa111 has addLabels: ['Suspected Junk'] and archive: true
msg_bbb222 has addLabels: ['Action Needed'] and archive: false
msg_ccc333 has addLabels: ['Informational'] and archive: false
msg_ddd444 (Unknown) is NOT included in the actions array — Unknown emails don't get labels
msg_eee555 has addLabels: ['Suspected Junk'] and archive: true
The actions array has exactly 4 entries (not 5, since Unknown is excluded)

evals.expected_output: The skill should identify the 2 failures, explain them to the user, and describe a retry strategy.
expectations: The skill identifies that msg_bbb222 and msg_eee555 failed
The skill reports the specific error messages: 'Message not found' for msg_bbb222 and 'Label creation failed' for msg_eee555
The skill proposes building a retry payload with just the 2 failed actions
The skill acknowledges that msg_aaa111 and msg_ccc333 succeeded and do NOT need to be retried
The skill mentions that if the retry also fails, the user may need to apply those labels manually

evals.prompt:
I've deployed the Apps Script and created email-triage-config.json in my working folder. Before triaging, please run the health check to confirm the Apps Script is reachable. Do NOT actually triage any emails — just verify the deployment is working.

Note for testing: Simulate that the health check Python command returns this response:
{"status":"ok","service":"gmail-calendar-actions","capabilities":["gmail","calendar","email"],"timestamp":"2026-02-21T10:30:00Z"}

Describe the exact steps you would take and how you interpret the response.
evals.expected_output: The skill should run a Python urllib.request GET to the Apps Script URL and interpret the response as a successful deployment.
expectations: The skill reads email-triage-config.json to get the Apps Script URL
The skill uses Python urllib.request via Bash to reach the Apps Script URL for the health check
The skill reads the command output to get the health check response
The skill interprets the response: status 'ok' means the deployment is working
The skill confirms the service name matches 'gmail-calendar-actions'
The skill reports to the user that the Apps Script deployment is healthy and ready

evals.prompt:
I've deployed the Apps Script but the health check is failing. When I run the health check, I get this response: Error

Script function not found: doGet

What's wrong and how do I fix it?
evals.expected_output: The skill should diagnose the issue as a missing doGet function or deployment problem and guide the user to fix it.
expectations: The skill identifies that the response is an HTML error, not the expected JSON health check
The skill diagnoses the problem: the doGet function is missing or the script was not deployed correctly
The skill suggests the user check that they pasted the complete script code including the doGet function
The skill suggests redeploying the web app (Deploy → Manage deployments → New version)
The skill references the troubleshooting section in the setup guide or provides equivalent advice

evals.expected_output: The skill should diagnose the secret mismatch and guide the user to fix it.
expectations: The skill identifies this as a shared secret mismatch between email-triage-config.json and the Apps Script
The skill advises the user to check that the apps_script_secret in email-triage-config.json matches the SHARED_SECRET variable in the Apps Script
The skill does NOT proceed to label emails or retry with the same secret
The skill suggests specific steps: open Apps Script editor, check the SHARED_SECRET value, compare with config file

evals.prompt:
After labeling emails via the Apps Script, I need to verify the labels were applied. The Apps Script reported all 5 actions succeeded. Now I run the verification query.

Simulate that search_gmail_messages with query 'in:inbox -label:Suspected-Junk -label:Action-Needed -label:Informational -label:RSS-/-Newsletters' returns these 3 emails:

1. messageId: 'msg_unknown1', From: mystery@example.com, Subject: 'Hello' — this was classified as Unknown (intentionally not labeled)
2. messageId: 'msg_missed1', From: spam@junk.com, Subject: 'Buy now!' — this was classified as Suspected Junk but the label is missing
3. messageId: 'msg_new1', From: boss@company.com, Subject: 'New meeting' — this is a NEW email that arrived after triage started (not in the classification list)

Describe the verification logic and what actions you would take.
evals.expected_output: The skill should correctly distinguish between intentionally unlabeled (Unknown), missed labels, and new emails. The verification query should include custom label exclusions.
expectations: The skill recognizes msg_unknown1 as an intentionally unlabeled Unknown classification — no action needed
The skill identifies msg_missed1 as a failed label application that needs to be fixed
The skill proposes re-sending msg_missed1 to the Apps Script with its original classification (Suspected Junk, archive: true)
The skill recognizes msg_new1 as a new email not in the original classification list — it should NOT be labeled in this session
The skill would re-verify after the retry to confirm msg_missed1 is now labeled
The skill reports verification results to the user including how many succeeded on first pass vs needed retry
The verification query includes custom label exclusions (e.g., -label:RSS-/-Newsletters) alongside the built-in label exclusions

evals.prompt:
I need to send a large action plan with 30 emails to the Apps Script. Show me how you would handle this — describe the exact approach for constructing and executing the request.

Assume the Apps Script URL is https://script.google.com/macros/s/EXAMPLE/exec and the secret is my-secret-key.
evals.expected_output: The skill should use Python urllib.request via Bash to POST the action plan, writing the JSON to a temp file first to avoid shell escaping issues.
expectations: The skill uses Python urllib.request via Bash to send the POST request
The skill writes the JSON payload to a temporary file rather than inlining it in the command
The skill sets Content-Type to 'application/json' in the request headers
The skill reads the JSON from the temp file and sends it as the request body
The skill prints the response body so it can be parsed
The skill explains that writing to a temp file avoids shell escaping issues with large JSON payloads

evals.expected_output: The skill should recognize this as a systemic issue rather than individual failures, and suggest troubleshooting steps.
expectations: The skill recognizes this as unusual — the Apps Script reported success but no labels were actually applied
The skill suggests the Apps Script may have a bug, or the message IDs may not be matching correctly
The skill suggests re-running the health check to confirm the script is still working
The skill considers that the Gmail API may have a propagation delay — suggest waiting briefly and re-verifying
The skill does NOT blindly retry all 8 emails repeatedly without investigating the root cause
The skill suggests the user check the Apps Script execution logs (View → Executions in the script editor) for errors

evals.prompt: Run the Apps Script health check. My email-triage-config.json is in the working folder. This is a LIVE test — actually run the health check command and read the response.
evals.expected_output: The skill should run a Python urllib.request GET to the Apps Script URL and confirm the health check returns a valid JSON response with status ok.
expectations: The skill reads email-triage-config.json to get the Apps Script URL
The skill uses Python urllib.request via Bash to reach the Apps Script URL
The skill reads the output and finds a JSON response containing 'status' and 'ok'
The skill confirms the service name is 'gmail-calendar-actions'
The skill reports that the health check passed and the deployment is live

evals.prompt:
Send a test POST to the Apps Script with an empty actions array to verify round-trip communication. My email-triage-config.json is in the working folder. This is a LIVE test — actually send the request via Python and read the response.

Use the real URL and secret from the config file. Send: {"secret": "<from config>", "actions": []}
evals.expected_output: The skill should use Python urllib.request to POST to the Apps Script and read back a valid JSON response.
expectations: The skill reads email-triage-config.json to get the URL and secret
The skill uses Python urllib.request via Bash to send the POST request
The skill sets Content-Type to 'application/json' in the request headers
The skill sends a JSON body with the correct secret and an empty actions array
The skill receives a successful response (no exceptions raised)
The response body contains valid JSON with 'processed' or 'actions_processed' equal to 0 and a message or errors field indicating 'No actions provided'
The skill reports the round-trip test succeeded

evals.prompt:
Send a test POST to the Apps Script with a WRONG secret to verify that authentication is enforced. My email-triage-config.json is in the working folder. This is a LIVE test — actually send the request via Python.

Use the real URL from the config file but send: {"secret": "wrong-secret-12345", "actions": []}
evals.expected_output: The skill should send the request and correctly interpret the authentication failure response from the Apps Script.
expectations: The skill reads email-triage-config.json to get the Apps Script URL
The skill uses Python urllib.request via Bash to send the POST with the wrong secret
The skill receives a response indicating authentication failure (e.g., 'Invalid or missing secret')
The skill correctly interprets this as a secret mismatch, not a network or deployment error
The skill does NOT report the health check or deployment as broken — the script is working, just rejecting the bad secret

evals.prompt:
I have the following emails in my inbox. Please classify each one according to my triage-rules.md (which is in the working folder). Do NOT use Gmail or Chrome tools — just classify based on the information I provide and present results in review mode format.

Email 1: From: writer@example.substack.com, Subject: 'The Weekly Deep Dive: AI Edition', Snippet: 'This week we explore the latest in artificial intelligence...' (contains unsubscribe link)

Email 2: From: noreply@mail.bloombergbusiness.com, Subject: 'Money Stuff: Hedge Funds Are Hedging', Snippet: 'Good morning. Today we discuss hedge fund strategies...' (contains unsubscribe link)

Email 3: From: noreply@substack.com, Subject: 'Your password has been changed', Snippet: 'The password for your Substack account was recently changed...'

Email 4: From: author@cooking.substack.com, Subject: 'New post: Summer Recipes', Snippet: 'Here are my favorite summer recipes...' (contains unsubscribe link)

Email 5: From: noreply@mail.bloombergbusiness.com, Subject: 'Bloomberg Markets: Daily Wrap', Snippet: 'Markets closed higher today...' (contains unsubscribe link, NOT Money Stuff)
evals.expected_output: Tests Custom Label classification for RSS: Newsletters — Substack newsletters and Money Stuff get the custom label, but Substack account notifications and non-Money-Stuff Bloomberg emails do not.
expectations: Email 1 (Substack newsletter from example.substack.com) is classified with custom label 'RSS: Newsletters' — matches *@substack.com pattern and is a newsletter post
Email 2 (Money Stuff from Bloomberg) is classified with custom label 'RSS: Newsletters' — matches Money Stuff criteria (Bloomberg sender with 'Money Stuff' in subject)

Email 3 (Substack password change) is NOT classified as 'RSS: Newsletters' — it's an account notification (password reset), which is an exception to the custom label rule. Should be classified with a built-in label instead
Email 4 (Substack newsletter from cooking.substack.com) is classified with custom label 'RSS: Newsletters' — matches *@substack.com pattern and is a newsletter post

Email 5 (Bloomberg Markets, NOT Money Stuff) is NOT classified as 'RSS: Newsletters' — the subject does not contain 'Money Stuff'. Should be classified as Suspected Junk via Rule 2 (newsletter with unsubscribe link)

evals.prompt:
I've classified these emails and now need to build the action plan for labeling. Do NOT actually run any commands or tools — just show me the JSON action plan you would send to the Apps Script. Here are the classifications:

1. messageId: 'msg_sub111', From: writer@example.substack.com, Label: RSS: Newsletters (archive)
2. messageId: 'msg_money222', From: noreply@mail.bloombergbusiness.com, Label: RSS: Newsletters (archive)
3. messageId: 'msg_spam333', From: spam@junk.com, Label: Suspected Junk (archive)
4. messageId: 'msg_boss444', From: boss@testcompany.com, Label: Action Needed
5. messageId: 'msg_unknown555', From: mystery@example.org, Label: Unknown

Assume the Apps Script URL is https://script.google.com/macros/s/EXAMPLE/exec and the secret is test-secret-456.
evals.expected_output: A correctly structured JSON action plan that includes custom label entries with proper archive behavior.
expectations: The JSON payload includes a 'secret' field set to 'test-secret-456'
msg_sub111 has addLabels: ['RSS: Newsletters'] and archive: true — custom label with archive behavior from triage-rules.md
msg_money222 has addLabels: ['RSS: Newsletters'] and archive: true — custom label with archive behavior
msg_spam333 has addLabels: ['Suspected Junk'] and archive: true
msg_boss444 has addLabels: ['Action Needed'] and archive: false
msg_unknown555 (Unknown) is NOT included in the actions array
The actions array has exactly 4 entries (not 5, since Unknown is excluded)
The custom label name 'RSS: Newsletters' is used exactly as defined in triage-rules.md (with the colon and space)

evals.prompt:
You just finished triaging my email. Here's what happened during the run:

- Processed 15 emails: 6 Suspected Junk, 2 Action Needed, 3 Informational, 1 RSS: Newsletters, 3 Unknown
- Apps Script POST succeeded (all labels applied)
- BUT verification found 2 emails (msg_abc from spam@junk.com 'Buy Now' and msg_def from promo@deals.com 'Flash Sale') that were classified as Suspected Junk but labels were NOT applied
- Retry succeeded for both after re-sending to the Apps Script
- All labels verified on second pass

Now perform Step 7 from the skill. Write the improvement-log.md entry you would create. Use the format from references/improvement-log-template.md.
evals.expected_output: The skill should write an error entry documenting the verification failure and retry.
expectations: The skill decides to write an entry because verification caught missing labels (an error occurred)
The entry has the correct format: date, title, category, run summary, what happened, what was tried, proposed fix, priority
The category is 'error' (label application failed and needed retry)
The 'What happened' section mentions the specific emails that failed (msg_abc and msg_def) and their subjects
The 'What was tried' section describes the retry and that it succeeded on the second pass
The 'Proposed fix' section suggests a concrete investigation or change (e.g., check if certain senders/subjects cause issues, monitor for recurrence, or check Apps Script logs)
The run summary mentions 15 emails processed with the label breakdown

evals.prompt:
You just finished triaging my email. Here's what happened during the run:

expectations: The skill decides to write an entry because the health check had a transient failure

The 'What happened' section describes the initial HTML error response from the health check
The 'What was tried' section mentions the retry after 10 seconds and that it succeeded
The 'Proposed fix' section suggests monitoring for recurrence, or notes that Apps Script cold starts can cause transient failures
